title: Collection and processing of intertidal seagrass tissues for bacterial and fungal culturing

steps: Put on gloves.
Walk to a location that is submerged (~0.5 m).
Using a coring device, take a sample of an individual seagrass plant and its associated sediment.
Place core in dark box On ice until transport back to the lab.
Replace gloves and repeat to obtain multiple cores.
Collect nearby seawater in 1 L Nalgene bottles (or similar) as needed for media recipes.
Using new gloves, carefully pull up additional seagrass plants as needed for media recipes and/or inoculation, place into sterile ziplock bags and then put On ice .
Once back at the lab, place cores, bulk tissues and seawater into 4 °C fridge or cold room and use as needed within 04:00:00 - 24:00:00 of collection.
Insert a clear PVC pipe with a 2.375 Inch diameter that has been modified such that one end of the pipe is cut at an angle to make insertion into the sediment easier.
Once inserted, Cap the PVC pipe, pull the core up and then cap the bottom of the core.

Plant tissues (leaf, root, rhizome, matte). Before starting, prepare plates with your favorite culture media (e.g. see Ettinger & Eisen 2020 for possible media choices previously used for seagrass-associated work) Rinse the tissue with autoclaved nanopure water to remove loosely associated sediment for ~ 00:00:30 .
Cut ~ 1 cm pieces of tissue using flame sterilized scissors
Place a subset of these tissue segments directly on plates using flame sterilized tweezers (1–3 segments/plate).
Take another subset of tissue segments and place these segments into 1.5 mL centrifuge tubes with 1 mL of autoclaved nanopure water.
Vortex the 1.5 mL centrifuge tubes for ~ 00:00:30 .
Directly plate intact rinsed tissue segments on media using flame sterilized tweezers (1–3 segments/plate).
Pipette 350 µL of wash liquid from rinsed segments or of homogenized smashed tissue directly on plates. As needed, you can also pipette 1:10 and 1:100 dilutions of wash liquid on plates.
If of interest, one can try to remove epiphytes from tissues through several wash steps before plating. One protocol is to bleach tissues prior to plating, for example:
For a subset of segments, homogenize using a sterile pestel, while for another subset leave the segments intact.
First, immerse segments for 00:05:00 in 1 mL 0.5% NaOCl (~10% bleach).
Then immerse in 1 mL of 95% EtOH for 00:01:00 .
Next immerse in 1 mL autoclaved nanopure water for 00:03:00 .
Finally directly plate intact bleached tissue segments on media using flame sterilized tweezers (1–3 segments / plate).
Another, slightly longer protocol to surface clean tissues, involves:
First, immerse segments in 500 µL 95% ethanol for ~ 00:00:05 .
Place sediment into 1.5 mL centrifuge tubes.
Add 1 mL of autoclaved nanopure water to tubes.
Vortex tubes for ~ 00:00:30 .
Pipette 350 µL of sediment suspension directly onto plates. As needed, you can also pipette 1:10 and 1:100 dilutions of sediment suspension on plates.
Pipette 350 µL of seawater directly onto plates. As needed, you can also pipette 1:10 and 1:100 dilutions of seawater on plates.
Then, immerse in 500 µL 0.5% NaOCl (∼10% bleach) for 00:02:00 .
Next, immerse in 500 µL 70% ethanol for 00:02:00 .
Finally, directly plate intact surface cleaned tissue segments on media using flame sterilized tweezers (1–3 segments /plate).
Then rinse segments with autoclaved nanopure water for 0w 0d 0h 1m 0s 00:01:00
creator: Cassie Ettinger
